section_title: INTRODUCTION TO OTE PATTERN RECOGNITION SERIES
content:
- VOL. 05
Welcome back to Volume 5 in the series of 20 videos from the ICT Optimal Trade Entry (OTE) Pattern
Recognition Series. This example focuses on the E-mini S&P June contract for 2020.
A previous example in this series revolved around the previous week's Friday's high, which presented an
opportunity and later gave another chance to trade into that level. Once price reached that level, the trade
idea was considered complete, having moved over 130 handles for the S&P, which is significant. The
overall framework leading up to the current setup for May 13, 2020, is bearish. The target for this example
is the previous day's low, specifically the low of May 12, 2020. The analysis will now transition to the 5
- minute chart.

section_title: 5-MINUTE CHART ANALYSIS AND OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY SETUP
content:
On the 5-minute chart, the previous day's low (May 12th low) is identified. Trading below this level would be
considered 'suicide liquidity'. The hourly chart had previously outlined a bearish market structure, indicating
a market intention to seek liquidity below this previous day's low.
Within the New York session, a setup is observed. Price trades down to a low and then begins retracing.
This retracement leads into a bearish order block, identified as the last up-close candle before the move
down. Price trades into the 'butt end' of this order block.
- Theoretical Entry: 2865
- Theoretical Stop: Above the high of the retracement

section_title: TARGET PROJECTIONS AND MANAGING PARTIAL PROFITS
content:
When dealing with the S&P, profit targets are measured in handles, not pips. The initial target for the
optimal trade entry was the range low, which offered over 30 handles on the S&P.
Subsequent partial profit targets are established:
- Second Partial: Plus 50 handles, taken at half of the projected standard deviation. This refers to half the
range from the high to the low of the observed price action.
- Third Partial: Plus 75 handles, taken at one full standard deviation projection of this range. This
projection would send price into approximately 2788 and three quarters. A 'beautiful response' was
observed at this level.

The entire setup and observed price action is fundamentally based on the liquidity resting below the
previous day's low. It is crucial to understand that the Fibonacci tool itself is not the 'magic' or the reason
why price is moving to these levels. Fibonacci has absolutely nothing to do with the underlying reasons for
price movement.
The core reason price moves down is the market's intention to seek that resting liquidity. It is important to
note that the market does not always achieve this in a single, direct run. It can take several passes to get
down to the target liquidity, as demonstrated in this example. After reaching the target, a retracement was
observed, and it is anticipated that price might return to grab the 'tape' back to the low of May 12th.
